topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery arrived just as I was trying to focus on the quarterly reports. A small, elegant bouquet of deep purple irises sat on my desk, the stems wrapped in simple twine. There was no note, just the vibrant, almost aggressive color of the blooms. My hand, hovering over the keyboard, froze mid-stroke. I picked up a single iris, its velvety texture cool beneath my fingertips. Every muscle in my neck felt coiled tight, a sudden, sharp awareness of being observed settling over me. Who would do this? The silence in the office suddenly felt amplified, every distant hum of the ventilation system sounding like a countdown. I stared at the flowers, a knot tightening low in my stomach, wondering if this was a gesture of kindness or a prelude to something much more complicated.

[story 2]

When the courier handed over the arrangement, my breath hitched. It was a riot of orange and yellow, so bright it seemed out of place against the muted grey of my apartment hallway. I took the box with a shaky grip, my knuckles whitening as I navigated the stairs. Inside, the flowers were perfectly arranged, undeniably beautiful, yet utterly devoid of any explanation. I set the box down on the worn wooden floor, my gaze darting around the room as if expecting someone to materialize from behind the sofa. My jaw was clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I paced three steps toward the window, my reflection in the glass looking strained and pale. A strange, unsettling flutter began in my chest, a feeling of being entirely exposed.

[story 3]

I found the flowers tucked into my mailbox this morning. They were lilies, pure white, their fragrance surprisingly overwhelming in the cool morning air. I didn't open the box immediately; instead, I stood by the door, listening intently for any sound, any indication that someone was waiting just around the corner. My fingers traced the smooth paper wrapping, the texture oddly yielding under my touch. Inside, the silence was absolute, broken only by the faint rustle of the wrapping paper. A quick, shallow intake of air escaped my lungs. My heart began to beat a rapid, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I forced myself to look at the arrangement, trying to anchor myself to the reality of the petals, desperate to keep my internal state from spiraling into something uncontrolled.
